A streamlined, modern design system built to scale across sector-specific products and accelerate development.

This project focused on reducing user mistakes in a high-risk transaction flow.

I redesigned form structure around user intent, improved confirmations, and clarified fee/timing language at key decision points. We tested multiple prototypes and iterated quickly with compliance stakeholders.

The shipped flow lowered transfer-related support requests while improving completion confidence.

Mapped end-to-end service touchpoints to improve completion and reduce administrative overhead.

The intake journey spanned digital forms, staff handoffs, and in-person checkpoints.

I mapped service touchpoints to find friction between systems and teams, then proposed an intake model with clearer status visibility and fewer redundant steps.

The result was improved form completion rates and less manual rework for front-desk staff.

Intelligence platform that helps fuel suppliers optimize supply, pricing, and customer performance, driving customer retention and opening new revenue opportunities in the energy sector.

**User Problem:** Analysts spent hours reconciling fragmented fuel data, couldn’t make timely trading or operational decisions. **Business Goal:** Launch a differentiated intelligence platform that helps fuel suppliers optimize supply, pricing, and customer performance, driving customer retention and opening new revenue opportunities in the energy sector ## Context Fuel suppliers often manage pricing, inventory, and customer performance across fragmented systems. Teams were forced to move between spreadsheets, BI exports, and internal tools to answer basic operational questions. The opportunity was to create a single intelligence hub that supported daily decisions while also giving leadership a clearer picture of retention risk and revenue opportunities. ![Placeholder: Product overview hero image or dashboard screenshot](/images/placeholder-thumb.svg) _Placeholder for a full-width hero screenshot of the hub homepage, key KPI row, or an annotated overview of the information architecture._ ## Problem Framing (Text + Image Split)

What was not working

  • Analysts spent too much time assembling data before they could act on it.
  • Account teams lacked a shared view of customer performance trends.
  • Pricing and supply decisions were made without clear confidence signals.
  • Leadership reporting was reactive and manually compiled.

Placeholder for a workflow diagram, pain-point map, or service blueprint excerpt.

## Design Direction (Alternating Image + Narrative Blocks)

1. Decision-first dashboard structure

I reorganized the experience around the core questions users needed to answer each day: where margin is at risk, which accounts need intervention, and where operational constraints could impact delivery.

Instead of presenting a flat analytics surface, the layout grouped metrics into action-oriented sections with progressive drill-downs.

Placeholder for an annotated interface showing hierarchy, KPI grouping, and drill-down entry points.

2. Customer health and opportunity signals

A new customer intelligence area surfaced retention and expansion indicators in one view. This helped account teams identify trend shifts earlier and coordinate with pricing/supply teams from the same signal set.

Placeholder for a customer detail panel with trend lines, risk flags, and opportunity signals.

User Problem: Analysts spent hours reconciling fragmented fuel data, couldn’t make timely trading or operational decisions.

Business Goal: Launch a differentiated intelligence platform that helps fuel suppliers optimize supply, pricing, and customer performance, driving customer retention and opening new revenue opportunities in the energy sector

Context

Fuel suppliers often manage pricing, inventory, and customer performance across fragmented systems. Teams were forced to move between spreadsheets, BI exports, and internal tools to answer basic operational questions.

The opportunity was to create a single intelligence hub that supported daily decisions while also giving leadership a clearer picture of retention risk and revenue opportunities.

Placeholder for a full-width hero screenshot of the hub homepage, key KPI row, or an annotated overview of the information architecture.

Problem Framing (Text + Image Split)

What was not working

  • Analysts spent too much time assembling data before they could act on it.
  • Account teams lacked a shared view of customer performance trends.
  • Pricing and supply decisions were made without clear confidence signals.
  • Leadership reporting was reactive and manually compiled.

Placeholder for a workflow diagram, pain-point map, or service blueprint excerpt.

Design Direction (Alternating Image + Narrative Blocks)

1. Decision-first dashboard structure

I reorganized the experience around the core questions users needed to answer each day: where margin is at risk, which accounts need intervention, and where operational constraints could impact delivery.

Instead of presenting a flat analytics surface, the layout grouped metrics into action-oriented sections with progressive drill-downs.

Placeholder for an annotated interface showing hierarchy, KPI grouping, and drill-down entry points.

2. Customer health and opportunity signals

A new customer intelligence area surfaced retention and expansion indicators in one view. This helped account teams identify trend shifts earlier and coordinate with pricing/supply teams from the same signal set.

Placeholder for a customer detail panel with trend lines, risk flags, and opportunity signals.

Before / After Comparison (Two Image Placeholders)

BeforeAfter
Dense analytics with weak hierarchy and unclear next steps.Decision-first layout with clearer prioritization and progressive disclosure.

Process Snapshot (Text-led Block)

Approach

  1. Mapped recurring operational decisions and the data needed to support each one.
  2. Audited existing analytics surfaces and identified hierarchy / navigation failures.
  3. Prototyped alternative dashboard structures and tested comprehension with users.
  4. Sequenced delivery into phases so high-value workflows shipped first.

Collaboration

  • Product: prioritization, roadmap sequencing, success metrics
  • Engineering: data availability, performance constraints, implementation strategy
  • Operations / Account teams: validation of workflows, terminology, and decision timing

Outcomes (Metrics + Supporting Visual Placeholder)

  • Reduced time-to-insight for recurring operational reviews
  • Improved clarity of customer performance and retention risk signals
  • Fewer support questions tied to dashboard interpretation
  • Stronger alignment between account, pricing, and supply stakeholders

Design Critiques That Actually Improve Work
A practical structure for critique sessions that sharpen decisions, not egos.

Critique quality is mostly a facilitation problem.

When sessions anchor on goals, evidence, and constraints, feedback becomes actionable instead of subjective. I use a lightweight format that separates observation, interpretation, and recommendation.

Teams leave with clearer decisions and faster iteration cycles.

Critique quality is mostly a facilitation problem.

When sessions anchor on goals, evidence, and constraints, feedback becomes actionable instead of subjective. I use a lightweight format that separates observation, interpretation, and recommendation.

Teams leave with clearer decisions and faster iteration cycles.
Simple moderation shifts can dramatically improve research signal quality.

Leading prompts create false confidence and weak product decisions.

I prefer neutral language, concrete follow-ups, and task reconstruction over opinion polling. This reveals real behavior and tradeoffs instead of aspirational answers.

A small interview guide rewrite often improves signal more than recruiting more participants.

When to Standardize and When to Bend the Rules
Consistency matters, but rigid systems can become friction points if misapplied.

Design systems scale quality, but they should not block meaningful problem-solving.

Standards work best when teams understand intent, not just component availability. For edge cases, intentional exceptions can preserve clarity and improve outcomes.

A healthy system balances guardrails with room for context.

Why Good UX Starts With Better Questions
Framing assumptions early helps teams avoid expensive design detours later.

Strong UX outcomes are often decided before pixels are explored. When teams align on problem framing, constraints, and success criteria, design work moves faster with less churn. Question quality is a leverage point: better questions produce better options. ## Why this matters Teams often treat design as the phase where answers appear. In practice, design quality is heavily shaped by the quality of the questions that come before solutioning starts. Weak questions create shallow options: - "Can we make this page simpler?" - "Can we add AI here?" - "Can we move faster on onboarding?" Better questions produce better design decisions: - What job is the user trying to complete, and where does progress currently stall? - Which constraints are real, and which are inherited assumptions? - What decision will this screen help a user make? ![Placeholder: question framing worksheet or whiteboard capture](/images/placeholder-thumb.svg) _Placeholder for a whiteboard photo, question map, or planning artifact._ ## A practical framing pattern (Text + Image Split)

Start with decision quality, not feature scope

Before exploring flows, I want clarity on the decisions the product must support. If a team can describe the decision, the actor, and the timing, the design space usually becomes easier to navigate.

  • What choice needs to be made?
  • Who is making it?
  • What information is required to make it confidently?
  • What happens if the decision is delayed or wrong?

Placeholder for a simple framing canvas that ties decisions, actors, and constraints together.

## The questions I ask before wireframes ### 1. What are we actually optimizing for? Teams often answer with a metric, but that is not enough. I also want to know what tradeoffs are acceptable and what quality bar matters to the business. ### 2. What would success look like in user behavior? A strong answer describes behavior change, not just shipped output. It tells us what "better" should feel like in the user journey. ### 3. Where is uncertainty highest right now? This is usually where research, prototypes, or instrumentation can save the most time later. ## Bad question vs better question (Comparison Block) | Common prompt | Better framing question | | --- | --- | | "Can we redesign onboarding?" | Which step in onboarding causes the highest abandonment, and what uncertainty causes it? | | "Can we make the dashboard cleaner?" | What decisions should the dashboard enable in the first 60 seconds? | | "Can we add more personalization?" | Which user segments need different defaults to complete the same job successfully? | ## Example: from vague request to useful brief (Alternating Blocks)

Initial request

"Users are confused by the reporting area. We should redesign it."

This statement may be true, but it is too broad to guide meaningful design choices. It bundles together comprehension, navigation, hierarchy, and expectation-setting into one vague problem.

Placeholder for early notes showing broad, unstructured problem language.

Refined framing

"Operations managers cannot identify which facilities need attention in under two minutes during morning review. They lack clear prioritization signals and have to scan too many charts to find risk."

This version gives design a usable target: who, when, what task, what failure mode, and what kind of information hierarchy is missing.

Placeholder for a clearer brief with actors, timing, decision, and constraints highlighted.

## Quick checklist for better questions - Define the user and the moment, not just the feature area - Name the decision the interface should support - Separate known constraints from assumptions - Clarify what tradeoffs are acceptable - Decide what evidence would change your mind ## A lightweight team exercise (Process Snapshot) 1. Write the problem statement in one sentence. 2. Underline every vague term (`better`, `cleaner`, `intuitive`, `smart`, `streamlined`). 3. Replace each vague term with observable behavior or a decision outcome. 4. List the top 3 constraints (technical, operational, regulatory, or timeline). 5. Agree on what will be measured after release. > Better design questions do not slow teams down. They reduce wasted motion by making the design work more directional from the start. ## Closing thought Design is often judged by artifacts, but the leverage starts earlier. The teams that ask better questions produce stronger options, make tradeoffs faster, and ship work that holds up longer.

Strong UX outcomes are often decided before pixels are explored.

When teams align on problem framing, constraints, and success criteria, design work moves faster with less churn.

Question quality is a leverage point: better questions produce better options.

Why this matters

Teams often treat design as the phase where answers appear. In practice, design quality is heavily shaped by the quality of the questions that come before solutioning starts.

Weak questions create shallow options:

  • “Can we make this page simpler?”
  • “Can we add AI here?”
  • “Can we move faster on onboarding?”

Better questions produce better design decisions:

  • What job is the user trying to complete, and where does progress currently stall?
  • Which constraints are real, and which are inherited assumptions?
  • What decision will this screen help a user make?

A practical framing pattern (Text + Image Split)

Start with decision quality, not feature scope

Before exploring flows, I want clarity on the decisions the product must support. If a team can describe the decision, the actor, and the timing, the design space usually becomes easier to navigate.

  • What choice needs to be made?
  • Who is making it?
  • What information is required to make it confidently?
  • What happens if the decision is delayed or wrong?

Placeholder for a simple framing canvas that ties decisions, actors, and constraints together.

The questions I ask before wireframes

1. What are we actually optimizing for?

Teams often answer with a metric, but that is not enough. I also want to know what tradeoffs are acceptable and what quality bar matters to the business.

2. What would success look like in user behavior?

A strong answer describes behavior change, not just shipped output. It tells us what “better” should feel like in the user journey.

3. Where is uncertainty highest right now?

This is usually where research, prototypes, or instrumentation can save the most time later.

Bad question vs better question (Comparison Block)

Common promptBetter framing question
”Can we redesign onboarding?”Which step in onboarding causes the highest abandonment, and what uncertainty causes it?
”Can we make the dashboard cleaner?”What decisions should the dashboard enable in the first 60 seconds?
”Can we add more personalization?”Which user segments need different defaults to complete the same job successfully?

Example: from vague request to useful brief (Alternating Blocks)

Initial request

"Users are confused by the reporting area. We should redesign it."

This statement may be true, but it is too broad to guide meaningful design choices. It bundles together comprehension, navigation, hierarchy, and expectation-setting into one vague problem.

Placeholder for early notes showing broad, unstructured problem language.

Refined framing

"Operations managers cannot identify which facilities need attention in under two minutes during morning review. They lack clear prioritization signals and have to scan too many charts to find risk."

This version gives design a usable target: who, when, what task, what failure mode, and what kind of information hierarchy is missing.

Placeholder for a clearer brief with actors, timing, decision, and constraints highlighted.

Quick checklist for better questions

  • Define the user and the moment, not just the feature area
  • Name the decision the interface should support
  • Separate known constraints from assumptions
  • Clarify what tradeoffs are acceptable
  • Decide what evidence would change your mind

A lightweight team exercise (Process Snapshot)

  1. Write the problem statement in one sentence.
  2. Underline every vague term (better, cleaner, intuitive, smart, streamlined).
  3. Replace each vague term with observable behavior or a decision outcome.
  4. List the top 3 constraints (technical, operational, regulatory, or timeline).
  5. Agree on what will be measured after release.

Better design questions do not slow teams down. They reduce wasted motion by making the design work more directional from the start.

Closing thought

Design is often judged by artifacts, but the leverage starts earlier. The teams that ask better questions produce stronger options, make tradeoffs faster, and ship work that holds up longer.

Prototyped interface cues that communicate confidence in recommendations without overwhelming users.

This concept focused on trust calibration in recommendation-heavy interfaces.

I tested lightweight confidence indicators, explanatory hover states, and reversible actions to reduce the fear of committing too early.

The resulting pattern set provided a clearer language for uncertainty while preserving a clean interaction flow.

Tested whether adaptive prompt sequencing could reduce onboarding friction in high-choice workflows.

This experiment explored how onboarding can change as users reveal intent signals.

Instead of one static walkthrough, the prototype adapted prompts based on initial actions and skipped irrelevant guidance.

The output was a rules framework for adaptive onboarding logic that can be reused across products with different complexity profiles.
